courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Management of Hospitality and Real Estate Businesses
courses.description:
HTMG1010 is an introductory course that takes a macro-level view of the hospitality, tourism, and real estate industries. The contents are geared towards students who have little to no industry experience but have expressed an interest in pursuing a career in the industry or a related field.

The primary objectives of this course are three-folded. First, this course discusses the historical developments, business models, and management structures of lodging, restaurant, managed service, casino/gaming, meetings / conventions / expositions, recreation, club, theme park, cruise, airline, and other hospitality and tourism organizations. Second, experiential learning opportunities are provided to help students understand the operations of hospitality organizations and the importance of service quality. The course also introduces students to the business world of real estate, including terminology, transaction process, issues, players and activities in the real estate industry. Third, the course prepares students for future development of leadership competencies with descriptions of the key management functions and the characteristics and practices of leaders and managers. Students will also learn about some of the most important business / tourism ethics principles and corporate social responsibilities.
courses.course_syllabus:
Part 1
Date Lecture Topic(s)
Week 1 Course Introduction and Overview
Week 2 The Hotel Business
Week 3 Hotel Food & Beverage Operation
Week 4 Rooms Division Operations – micro perspective
Week 5 The Restaurant operations – micro perspective
Week 6 Recreation, Attractions, and Clubs
Week 7 Meetings, Conventions/Exhibitions and Event Management
Week 8 Leadership and Management
Week 9 The Gaming Entertainment Industry

Part 2
Date Lecture Topic(s)
Week 10 Overview of real estate business
Week 11 Real estate development process
Week 12 Commercial real estate
Week 13 Final Exam
courses.feedback_evaluation: Class evaluation will be conducted at the end of the course
courses.required_readings: Walker, John R. (2014). Introducing Hospitality (6th Edition). Pearson Education Ltd.
courses.recommended_readings: Miles, M. E., et al. (2000). Real Estate Development: Principle and Process (3rd Edition). Urban Land Institute.

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Distinguished Speaker Series I
courses.description: The first course in the series. To keep abreast of the development that affects current managerial practices, industry executives of the hospitality, tourism, real estate and service related industries are invited as speakers to address the opportunities and challenges facing their respective fields. These courses provide a unique opportunity for successful industry leaders to share their visions, experiences and views regarding successful management styles, critical industry-related issues, identify career paths and offer career aspirations to the students in the programme.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Business concepts and functions
- Successful industry leaders to share their visions, experiences, and views regarding the recent development of management practices and critical issues in the hospitality industry
- Understand the hospitality and tourism industry, key trends and issues impacting the industry, and management practices used

Analytical skills
- Improve critical thinking and writing skills through reflection papers about the guest speakers

Professional Skills
- Successful industry leaders to share their visions, experiences, and views regarding the recent development of management practices and critical issues in the hospitality industry

Attitude/Values
- Successful industry leaders to share their own career paths and offer career aspirations to the students
courses.course_syllabus:
The field of hospitality and tourism management is a fast-paced, ever changing industry that requires tireless dedication, strong commitment, positive attitude and keen knowledge. Those who make it to top positions of leadership possess these abilities and traits, among others.

The Distinguished Speaker Series provides a unique opportunity for successful industry leaders to share their visions, experiences, and views regarding the recent development of management practices and critical issues in the hospitality industry. The speakers also share their own career paths and offer career aspirations to the students in the program. Upon completion of this course, students should have a better understanding of the hospitality and tourism industry, key trends and issues impacting the industry, and management practices used. Students will also improve their critical thinking and writing skills through reflection papers they will write about the guest speakers.
courses.feedback_evaluation: Class evaluation will be conducted at the end of the course
courses.required_readings: Nil
courses.recommended_readings: Subject to the recommendations by invited speakers.

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: International Experience
courses.description: An international experience is a personal and professional growth opportunity for students to interact with a culture other than their own, to improve their cultural understanding, and to prepare for working more effectively in diverse cultural and business environments. International experience has been shown to increase graduates’ employability. It is particularly important in global industries such as the hospitality and real estate industries where communication skills, problem-solving skills, and service orientation are crucial for career success. This course is designed to encourage students to participate in international experience activities prior to graduation. These activities include overseas exchange, internship, study tour, volunteer/service learning projects, and other international learning activities.
courses.course_syllabus:
By completing at least one of the following outside-Hong Kong learning activities offered or endorsed by the University, the CUHK Business School, College, and/or SHTM:
• Overseas study exchange program
• Overseas internship
• Overseas study tour
• Overseas short-term study (e.g., summer course)
• Overseas volunteer/service learning project

Timely submission IE Form with accurate information (including personal reflection report)

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Food and Beverage Management
courses.description: This is a course that examines the principles of food and beverage management. It begins with an overview of the food service industry and the specific disciplines of food service distribution. Current issues in food safety and sanitation procedures that affect managerial decisions in hospitality industry will be discussed. Detailed considerations are given to the components of the food service system: marketing, menu planning, purchasing, logistical support, guest service, controls, and quality assurance. Students will also learn through visits to restaurant operations, analyse the restaurant's concept (market), organization, ownership, management, physical structure, staff, front- and back-of-the-house operations, and fiscal integrity.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Pre-requisite: HTMG1010
courses.learning_outcomes:
Business Concepts and Functions
This course has two missions and they are aligned with an eight course paradigm:
1/ To enable students to discover all aspects in opening, running and owning a restaurant, from an entrepreneur perspective, and
2/ Students who would like to be future leaders in the F&B and hospitality industries, learn how do senior executives in strategic business units manage their business with the assistance of “P&L” (profit & loss statement)

Analytical Skills (Finance)
The bread and butter of F&B business are sales, gross profit, payroll control and gross operating profit. To support student’s learning, we share with students: the breakdown of “top line” with cover check; how to identify managerial/ operational problems from a P&L statement and come up with improvement plan; application of Solver Model to maximize production profit, and to plan appropriate labour coverage

Professional Skills (Operations, Marketing)
The spectrum of F&B learning is wide, from terminology of “mise-en-place” & conventional system; various cooking methods; HACCP; environmental health; carbon emission trade; facilities plan; beauty of technology - “sous vide” and “liaison froide”; to guerrilla marketing. Students should understand that the inventions and systems are custom-made to assist today’s management to protect /benefit our stakeholders at all cost.

Attitude/Values (Human Resources)
To arouse students’ interests, to embrace learning, students must be passionate about F&B. Students will experience sensory evaluation of classical cuisine and learn basic food and wine pairing knowledge.

F&B is a Human Capital Business and students will be tested individually, as well as in a team. Mid-term includes a team cooking test in our demonstration kitchen with time constraint.

Working with people to achieve goal is the prerequisite of future leader.
courses.feedback_evaluation: Class evaluation will be conducted at the end of the course
courses.required_readings: alacio, J. P., Harger, V., Shugart, G. and Theis, M. (2009). Introduction to Foodservice, Eleventh Edition. Pearson Prentice Hall, New Jersey.
courses.recommended_readings:
• Mill, R.C. (2007). Restaurant Management: Customers, Operations and Employees, Third Edition, Prentice-Hall Inc., New Jersey.
• Walker, J.R. and Lundberg, D.E. (2005). The Restaurant: From concept to operation. Fourth Edition, John Wiley and Sons, Inc., New Jersey.
• Contemporary readings will also be assigned from the Web and from traditional sources (e.g., academic journals, news articles from newspapers, the trade press, and other periodicals) throughout the semester.

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Summer Internship I
courses.description: In order for students in the Major to apply theory to application, they are required to gain hands-on experience and to participate in industry-related internship experiences throughout their study, preferably during two summers. Summer Internship I is a course to monitor students in their first summer internship training. The educational objectives can be achieved through industry activities that are either operational based and/or project based. The Internship Program allows students to gain a realistic expectation of the hospitality and tourism operations. All internship experiences must be approved by the School. Thus, for students who choose to self-place, it is his/her responsibility to seek prior written approval from the Director before the internship begins.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Pre-requisite: HTMG1010
courses.course_syllabus:
Hotel and Tourism Management majors are required to gain hands-on experience and to participate in industry-related projects during the summers of their second and third years of attendance. The internship can be either operational based or project based. The School has developed relationships with employers who consistently offer summer internships. A student though must seek approval from the School if they would like to arrange their own internship. The program carries credit (HTMG2900 & HTMG3900) which is a requirement for graduation. Both Internship I and II serve as pre-requisites for HTMG4900. That is, the enrolment in HTMG4900 is subject to the successful completion of the two summer internships requirements.

Before the internship, all students are required to participate in the pre-internship workshops prior to their first summer break. Pre-internship training will be provided for students to acquire the business etiquette, resume writing, interview skills and interpersonal skills required for succeeding in a workplace.
courses.feedback_evaluation: Class evaluation will be conducted at the end of the course
courses.required_readings:
• Guiding your internship: a hospitality/tourism manual, Mary Walk, Nancy Pike, Prentice-Hall, 1989
• Hospitality Management Internship: A Student Workbook, Kimberly J. Harris, Florida State University, 2005.
courses.recommended_readings: Nil

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Management of Lodging Facilities
courses.description: This course introduces students to the principles of hotel management. It contains an overview of organization, function, and management of lodging operation. The discussion will focus on the inter-relationships among stakeholders of a lodging facility - guests, managers, owners and employees. Special emphasis will be on the integration of the various departments of a hotel facility, i.e. Rooms, Front Desk, Sales, Food and Beverage (F&B), Purchasing, Housekeeping, Engineering and Maintenance, etc., to ensure customer satisfaction while maximizing the return on investment. Students will be exposed to computing applications specific to the hotel industry. Hotel security, guest safety as well as total quality issues will also be discussed.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Prerequisite: HTMG1010
courses.learning_outcomes:
•Identify the general manager’s major responsibilities in managing a hotel and understand the relationships among different department of a lodging property.
•Understand the fundamental processes of front office management.
•Examine the role of security department in protecting the assets of a hotel and safety of guests.
•Understand the basics of revenue management (a method for profitably managing hotel capacity), including forecasting demand, setting overbooking level, and using linear programming approach to optimized hotel revenue.
•Be aware of the information systems for hotel management and gain hands-in experience on popular hotel systems offered at the training center.
courses.course_syllabus:
to provide students with the skills and knowledge necessary to successfully operate a full-service hotel.
- - to provide students with the fundamental “ hows” and “ whys “ of management in the lodging industry.
- -to cover all key departments in a lodging property so that students will have a better understanding of how different departments fit together and contribute to its success.
- -to promote critical thinking among students by exposing them to theories, research based materials, actual case studies pertaining to hotel management.
- -to train students on acquiring the essential skills and knowledge of managing the front office and the various management systems for the successful operation of the property.
courses.feedback_evaluation: Class evaluation will be conducted at the end of the course

2. Kimes, Sheryl E. and Richard B. Chase. 1998. "The strategic levers of yield management, " Journal of Service Research, 1(2): 14-19
3. Lawrence R Weatherford, Sheryl E Kimes, Darren A Scott. 2001. " Forecasting for hotel revenue management: Testing aggregation against disaggregation," Cornell Hotel and Restaurant Administration Quarterly, 42(4): 53-64
4. Eric B Orkin. 1998. " Wishful thinking and rocket science: The essential matter of calculating unconstrained demand of revenue management," Cornell Hotel and Restaurant Administration Quarterly, 39(4): 15-19

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Hospitality Organization Behaviour
courses.description: All organizations are faced with “people issues.” Why do people think, feel, and act in certain ways in the workplace? How do you manage people effectively to achieve the organizational objectives? More important, how do you continue to manage people effectively when the organization is going through significant strategic/structural changes? This course draws upon the theoretical insights and empirical evidence from the Organizational Behavior (OB) research and introduces students to the applications of psychological and behavioral approaches to designing effective human resource management (HRM) policies and practices. The course provides a systematic overview of fundamental OB concepts, theories, and applications in the context of hospitality, tourism, and real estate businesses. We will discuss a variety of topics at three levels. First, we will study how individuals differ in their personality traits, values, attitudes, emotions, perceptions, and motivations. Second, we will examine how people interact with one another and work in groups and teams. Topics range from team dynamics, group decision making, power and influence, to conflict management. Leadership theories and practices will also be discussed. Third, we will discuss how organizational-level factors such as organizational culture and change affect attitudes, behaviors, and work performances of employees. The OB concepts and theories are closely related to HRM. Through the course students will learn how to apply OB theories to improve HRM effectiveness in a variety of areas, e.g., job design and analysis, employee recruitment and selection, training and socialization, employee performance appraisal, compensation and benefits, and organizational culture formation and maintenance. Students will also learn what characteristics, behaviors, attitudes, and competencies define good leadership and how to ensure successful organizational changes through effectively motivating employees. Overall, this course explains why and how individuals and groups are motivated to do their jobs and helps students develop critical leadership skills to manage people and organizational changes.
courses.enrollment_requirement: PR-MGNT2040/ HTMG1010/ HTMG3010
courses.learning_outcomes:
• To understand major concepts and theories in OB
• To apply OB concepts and theories to human resource management practices in hospitality and real estate organizations
• To understand characteristics, behaviors, attitudes, and competencies that define good leadership
• To apply OB concepts and theories to managing changes in organizations
• To develop critical thinking, problem solving, communication, and teamwork skills
courses.course_syllabus: This is an introductory course that integrates organizational behavior (OB) insights with HRM practices in hospitality and real estate organizations. We live in an organizational world. Studying OB (i.e., to understand how employees and managers behave and why they behave the way they do) helps you succeed in your careers and helps your organizations succeed. This course will help by covering a wide variety of OB topics such as personality traits, perception and decision making, motivation, team dynamics, leadership, power and influence, conflict resolution, and organizational culture and change.
courses.feedback_evaluation: Class evaluation will be conducted at the end of the course
courses.required_readings: Robbins, S. P. & Judge, T. A. (2019) Organizational Behavior (18th ed). Pearson.
courses.recommended_readings: Kotter, J. P. (2012) Leading Change. Harvard Business Review Press.

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Hospitality Real Estate Economics
courses.description: This course is designed to encourage students to think about hospitality properties as real estate. The two fundamental markets that are relevant to commercial real estate investment analysis are space market and asset market. The former refers to the market for the right to use real estate (e.g., land and buildings) and the latter refers to the market for the ownership of real estate assets. This course focuses on the space market. Specific objectives are: to recognize the importance of real estate space market in the overall real estate system, which involves an understanding of supply, demand and rent in the space market; to understand the fundamental factors of location of economic activities; and to learn real estate market analysis for providing advice to real estate decision makers.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Prerequisite: HTMG1010
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of this course, students will be able to:
- Describe the key concepts and fundamental importance of space market in the overall real estate system.
- Apply theoretical frameworks to real world, especially hospitality real estate industry.
- Critically analyze the real estate space market to make informed real estate decisions.
- Demonstrate capability in using quantitative skills.
- Develop creative thinking, self-managed ability, team work spirit, analytical and communication skills through class discussions and project presentations.
courses.course_syllabus:
It is a one-semester course to provide a conceptual and theoretical foundation for real estate space market with the applications to hospitality industry. This course primarily examines urban economics, which is the main discipline for studying real estate space market. Two main topics to be covered are the principles of urban economics and real estate market analysis.

The principles of urban economics deal with central place theory, system of cities, fundamental factors of location, concept of rent, highest and best use, bid-rent curve, and formation of land market.

The real estate market analysis deals with the analysis of market supply and demand indicators, and the formal model of space market equilibrium dynamics and cyclicality.
courses.feedback_evaluation: Class evaluation will be conducted at the end of the course
courses.required_readings:
1. Geltner, D.M., Miller, N.G., Clayton, J. and Eichholtz, P. (2014) Commercial Real Estate: Analysis and Investments, 3rd edition. USA: Oncourse Learning.
2. Brett, D.L. and Schmitz, A. (2009) Real Estate Market Analysis: Methods and Case Studies, 2nd edition. Washington, D.C.: Urban Land Institute.
courses.recommended_readings: Journal of Urban Economics; Journal of Real Estate Research; Real Estate Economics; Journal of Real Estate Finance and Economics

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Law for Hospitality and Real Estate Industry
courses.description: Knowing laws that govern the hospitality and real estate industries is no longer a luxury. It is a knowledge that every manager must acquire to protect their companies from unnecessary fines, lawsuits and negative publicity. This module focuses on the laws relating to the hospitality and real estate industry in Hong Kong. It provides students with the essential knowledge on legal principles that are important for the practice within the hospitality and real estate industry. The course will introduce a wide range of legal topics including legal system, company law, contract law, tort law and land law.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Prerequisite: HTMG1010
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of this course, students will be able to:
• Explain the basic legal principles in law related to business, company contract, tort, land and other relevant topics within the hospitality and real estate industry.
• Evaluate the cases which may be come across in the hospitality and real estate industry.
• Demonstrate the knowledge in the basic framework and legal principles in business, land and other related topics in the hospitality and real estate industry.
• Understand the complexity of the legal regime in the hospitality and real estate industry and identify the potential legal issues in managerial decision making.
• Solve problems by means of the application of the legal principles mentioned above.
• Develop self-managed ability, team work spirit, communication skills through case studies and project presentations.

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Travel and Tourism Management
courses.description: This course is an overview of the travel and tourism industry's management issues, professional opportunities, marketing concepts, planning and development strategies and its relevance to the geography of travel, transportation issues and travel service. Different aspects of tourism, including origin and destination flow models, tourist profiles, demand and supply interaction, and barriers to travel are explored and analysed. Emerging concepts such aseco-tourism, heritage tourism, adventure tourism, sustainable tourism, etc., will also be examined.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Pre-requisite: HTMG1010
courses.learning_outcomes:
Business concepts and functions
- - Understand the components of both demand-side and supply-side of tourism and the associated difficulties and issues
- - Understand the concept of marketing for tourism and related techniques

Analytical skills
- - Compare the components of the travel and tourism system
- - Analyze the future development of the tourism system

Professional Skills
- - Apply the knowledge acquired in Hong Kong as well as other regions in the world

Attitude/Values
- - Appreciate the economic impacts of tourism activities

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Hospitality Service Management and Innovation
courses.description:
Hospitality is a service industry which heavily rely on effective service management for competitive advantage. Service management is a well-established academic field which recognize service through integration of technology, operations and human behaviour. This course emphasizes the uniqueness of service management applicable to the hospitality industry and the need for continuous improvement in quality to ensure competitiveness in a global environment.
Topics include understanding of the new service economy concept, service strategy with data analytics and application of IoT in hospitality industry. Students will evaluate services designed through UX interface, analyze hospitality service operations, discuss the disruptive impact of technology and take part in innovate a service system/process applicable to the hospitality industry.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Pre-requisite: HTMG1010
courses.learning_outcomes:

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Real Estate Finance
courses.description: This course exposes students to concepts, theories and industry practice in real estate finance which is one of the largest and most important components of the financial system. It also introduces valuation and appraisal methods used for real estate finance and investment decision making. The primary goal of this course is to help students acquire the knowledge and develop the analytical skills of real estate finance, including the institutions and instruments used to finance residential and commercial real estate. It is of particular use to students who are interested in working the real estate development, brokerage, mortgage banking and investment banking industries. The main topics covered in the course are mortgage products, mortgage banking, secondary mortgage market and mortgage-backed securities (MBS), financial leverage and commercial real estate finance.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Prerequisite: FINA2010 or HTMG3030
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of this course, students will be able to:
- Differentiate and analyze different types of real estate (commercial, industrial, residential)
- Identify different types of mortgages available in the marketplace and explain their characteristics
- Understand and apply generally recognized investment valuation techniques to real estate
- Calculate fixed-rate mortgages (FRM) and adjustable-rate mortgages (ARM) (e.g. monthly payment, amortization and effective borrowing cost) and make a rational choice among various mortgages based on the calculations
- Identify the major players in the secondary mortgage market and explain how the market is functioning
- Understand and be able to explain the structure and the role of securitization in the property market
- Compare different sources of commercial real estate financing (both the debt side and the equity side) and analyze how leverage affects risk and return
- Conduct real estate investment analysis and make investment recommendations accordingly
courses.course_syllabus:
- Legal foundation of real estate finance
- Mathematics of real estate finance
- Mortgage instruments: Fixed rate mortgages
- Mortgage instruments: Adjustable rate mortgages
- Incremental borrowing cost, loan refinancing
- Residential mortgage banking
- Mortgage origination and underwriting
- Secondary mortgage market
- Mortgage-backed securities
courses.feedback_evaluation: Class evaluation will be conducted at the end of the course
courses.required_readings: 1. Brueggeman, W. B. and Fisher, J.D. (2011). Real Estate Finance and Investments. 14th edn. New York: McGraw-Hill. ISBN:978-0-07-337733-9.

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Wine Culture and Appreciation
courses.description: Wine has its roots customs, traditions and history. It occupies an important place in culture and life of people worldwide. Starting in the 1990s, there is a great surge of interest in wine across the globe. Wine has been a significant element in social gathering and business meetings. Wine becomes the challenge for hospitality operators to meet the needs of increasing sophisticated tastes of consumers. To be an effective hospitality manager, knowledge of the alcoholic products, particularly wine, is essential. The ability to taste, understand, evaluate, and articulate wine in a clear, easily understood manner is key to meeting customers' needs and at the same time, increasing wine sales and profitability. The course is designed to expose students wine label terminology, wine appreciation, and learn the principles on how to choose wines to pair with many different foods. The topics include cultural history of wine, the role of alcohol in society; major wine regions, grape varieties, vintages and winemaking process, sensory training and comparative tasting methods to help build students' ability to discern distinct flavours from major types of wine and build strong base for lifelong wine appreciation and enhance their quality of life.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon successful completion of this course, students will have attained the knowledge necessary to appreciate, select, serve, and manage wines with confidence. Specifically, this course will achieve the following learning outcomes:
I. Knowledge skill outcome:

1. Students will understand the process of vine growing and wine making. They will be exposed to what makes one wine different from another where sensory evaluation training and comparative tasting methods will help build their ability to discern distinct flavors from major types of wine.
2. Students will appreciate the major wine growing regions, quality standard and indigenous grape varietals.
II. Professional skill outcome:

3. Students will learn the principles on wine and food pairing for a balanced wine menu and factors to consider in crafting a selling wine list that fits the market position, food, and style of the establishment.
4. Students will learn how to determine wine quality characteristics based on geographical characteristics, historic and cultural context.
courses.course_syllabus:
Wine has its roots customs, traditions and history. It occupies an important place in culture and life of people worldwide. Starting in the 1990s, there is a great surge of interest in wine across the globe. Wine has been a significant element in social gathering and business meetings. It becomes the challenge for hospitality operators to meet the needs of increasing sophisticated tastes of consumers. To be an effective hospitality manager, knowledge of the alcoholic products, particularly wine, is essential. The ability to taste, understand, evaluate, and articulate wine in a clear, easily understood manner is key to meeting customers' needs and at the same time, increasing wine sales and profitability.

The course is designed to expose students wine label terminology, wine appreciation, and learn the principles on how to choose wines to pair with many different foods. The topics include cultural history of wine, the role of alcohol in society; major wine regions, grape varieties, vintages and winemaking process, sensory training and comparative tasting methods to help build students' ability to discern distinct flavors from major types of wine. This course aim at building strong base for lifelong wine appreciation and enhance students’ quality of life.

The course is oriented toward HRE majors who will be working in hospitality and real estate industries. The content of the course is designed to help students understand basic knowledge of wine from growing, making, tasting to serving. Students will learn how to select wines for a balanced menu and how to match various wines with food items. In addition, students will be exposed to what makes one wine different from another, how to evaluate wines and they will gain a geographical understanding of various wines.

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Shopping Mall Investment and Management
courses.description: Shopping mall business is an attractive business in Hong Kong. There are many leading shopping malls in the region and it generates stable rental income and attractive returns to the property developers. Property developers differentiate their shopping malls from their competitors and it forms a significant part of investment (called investment properties) on the Statement of Financial Positions and has significant impact on the Income Statement. This course aims at introducing students to the management and investment perspective of shopping mall business and how it contributes to tourism. It includes design of a shopping mall, its marketing, leasing process, customer services, car park management, investment return analysis, investment value calculation and the regulatory framework. Ethical considerations facing property developers and investors will also be discussed. After taking this course, students should have good understanding of the shopping mall business and allow them to work in the real estate management and investment field.

The leasing process

Parking issue in a shopping mall

What is investment property
How to enhance the value of a shopping mall
Regulatory framework of shopping mall business
Case study one
Case study two

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Hospitality Revenue Management and Analytics
courses.description: This course introduces both the theory and the practice of revenue management. Revenue management (RM) is the process of improving corporate revenue through the implementation of demand forecasting, strategic pricing and inventory/duration control. In a simple way, the concept of revenue management is to sell the right product to the right customer for the right price at the right time and through the right channel. In this course, we will focus on the important elements leading up to effective revenue management – market segmentation, differential pricing, demand forecasting, inventory control, distribution channel management and performance evaluation. RM has been a data and analytics heavy driven discipline. Students will be exposed and learn to apply various analytics techniques for revenue/price optimization.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Deepen their appreciation of the significance of revenue management in the global hospitality industry
2. Understand the basic concepts, processes and techniques of revenue management
3. Apply differential pricing, demand forecasting and inventory/duration control techniques to solve problems
4. Critically evaluate different measures of revenue management effectiveness
5. Conduct group displacement analysis
6. Improve group-communication and teamwork skills
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction to Revenue Management

2. Understand customer behaviour

3. The Market and Segmentation

4. Pricing and value Perception

5. Forecasting

6. Channel and Inventory Management

7. Internal Assessment & Competitive Analysis

8. Online marketing and online reputation

9. Distribution channel management

10. Data Analytic tools and tactics

11. Legal and ethical leadership
courses.feedback_evaluation: Class evaluation will be conducted at the end of the course

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Land Conversion Process and Development Control
courses.description:
Land improvement refers to investments that make land more usable for humans. Prior to the development of its industrial and commercial activities, Hong Kong was once an agrarian society with a large amount of active agricultural land. The growth of Hong Kong’s economy is accompanied with large areas of agricultural land, with fish ponds and farmlands, being developed into other uses. Land conversion process and development control has significant economic and social implications for all stakeholders involved.

This course is designed to address two major topics: lease control and statutory control during land conversion and development processes. The course provides a foundation for understanding land development. Landowners and developers of all sizes typically aim to maximize profits, minimize risks and control cash flow. These objectives require landowners and developers to design optimal plans for local markets and meet local legal and administrative requirements and procedures. This course introduces Hong Kong’s land tenure system and development control and helps students understand the geographical characteristics of real estate properties in Hong Kong that have been developed into different uses over time. Students will also learn about the interplay between development processes and local conditions.

Prerequisite of HTMG students: HTMG3030/4060/4100
Prerequisite of non-HTMG students: FINA2010/3080

• Land leasehold system
• Town planning
• Development control in Hong Kong
• Statutory control in Hong Kong
• Current land administration and development control

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Introduction to Real Estate Investments
courses.description:
This course provides the most fundamental concepts, principles and techniques useful for making investment decisions regarding commercial real estate assets. Important subjects such as discounted cash flow (DCF), net present value (NPV), valuation of property price, use of debt and tax investment analysis will be covered. Adequate time will be dedicated to learn how to build a fully integrated property financial model which is the most typical analytical tool to evaluate investment performance and help decision making. Students will walk through the financial details relevant to a property and create five-year/ten-year cash flow projections. Familiarity with excel will be a requirement of this course.

To enhance the mastery of the lecture and improve applications to real world practice, a number of case studies and practical exercises that help to reinforce the financial modelling techniques necessary for financial decision making will be selected.

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Designing and Managing Customer Experience
courses.description: This course provides an in-depth understanding to students on how hospitality companies can effectively manage and design customer experience online and offline to foster long-term customer relationships. Main topics include the unique characteristics of services and value co-creation; how to evaluate the service process using design thinking tools; how to engineer authentic and memorable customer experience with design thinking methodologies; how to handle customer complaints and perform service recoveries; and how to develop omnichannel strategy to engage with customers.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Prerequisite: MKTG2010
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of the course, students will be able to:

1. Deepen their appreciation on the characteristics of services and experiences and the unique business challenges they pose.

2. Understand the critical processes of managing customer experience for service organizations such as service design, service delivery to service recovery.

3. Construct a design brief to create valued experiences and productive operations; evaluate the experience using design thinking tools; and identify the challenges & solutions in meeting customer expectations.

4. Categorize, review, and evaluate industry’s use of omnichannel strategy to engage customers online and offline.

5. Improve team communication and negotiation skills.

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Summer Internship II
courses.description: In order for students in the Major to apply theory to application, they are required to gain hands-on experience and to participate in industry-related internship experiences throughout their study, preferably during summers. Summer Internship II is a course to monitor students in their second summer internship training. The objectives of the internship program are to 1) To provide management training opportunities for students to apply what they have learned in the classroom to solving real-world business problems related to the hospitality and tourism industry; 2) To develop a good understanding of the different aspects of the hospitality and tourism operations in Hong Kong and abroad; and 3) To strengthen and build character and instill professional attitude and passion in students towards the hospitality and tourism industry. Students will develop their analytical and problem solving skills by assuming the role of the manager, to offer probable recommendations/solutions to the operational problem that they encounter in the internship. All internship experiences must be approved by the School. Thus, for students who choose to self-place, it is his/her responsibility to seek prior written approval from the Director before the internship begins.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Pre-requisites: HTMG1010 and HTMG2900
courses.course_syllabus:
Hotel and Tourism Management majors are required to gain hands-on experience and to participate in industry-related projects during the summers of their second and third years of attendance. The internship can be either operational based or project based. The School has developed relationships with employers who consistently offer summer internships. A student though must seek approval from the School if they would like to arrange their own internship. The program carries credit (HTMG2900 & HTMG3900) which is a requirement for graduation. Both Internship I and II serve as pre-requisites for HTMG4900. That is, the enrolment in HTMG4900 is subject to the successful completion of the two summer internships requirements.

Before the internship, all students are required to participate in the pre-internship workshops prior to their first summer break. Pre-internship training will be provided for students to acquire the business etiquette, resume writing, interview skills and interpersonal skills required for succeeding in a workplace.
courses.feedback_evaluation: Class evaluation will be conducted at the end of the course

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Global Real Estate Asset Management
courses.description: Real estate is largely known as an alternative investment in a portfolio. It is primarily viewed as return-enhancing and risk-reducing portfolio investments. It is thus important to understand the performance of real estate in an investment portfolio. This course provides theoretical foundation for real estate asset allocation with applications to private and public real estate investments globally. Topics include real estate as an alternative investment, private real estate investments, publicly traded real estate securities, private equity funds, different types of asset allocation, capital asset pricing model (CAPM) and modern portfolio theory (MPT). To take this course, students are required to have completed the relevant prerequisite courses in real estate finance and investment. After taking this course, students will be able to choose the best performance assets in the investment portfolio.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Prerequisite: HTMG3523 & 4100
courses.learning_outcomes:
·Understand the considerations of asset allocation for individual and institutional investors

·Determine the value of real estate securities

·Apply international CAPM and MPT in real estate asset allocation analysis

·Discuss basic strategic and tactical strategies of real estate portfolio

·Discuss basic features of real estate derivatives and their applications in investment portfolio
courses.course_syllabus:
·Real estate as an alternative investment

·Private real estate investments

·Publicly traded real estate securities (i.e. listed real estate companies, REITs)

·Private equity valuation

·Different type of asset allocation

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Hospitality and Real Estate Financial Reporting and Analysis
courses.description: Hospitality and real estate entities encounter various financial reporting issues that could affect their performance measurement. In hospitality and real estate industries, the financial reporting is in fact unique and complex. While the industries have their own specific standards, it is mandatory that these entities also need to follow the statutory reporting requirements. This course covers the financial reporting practices adopted in the hospitality and real estate industries and applies the financial statement analysis techniques in the performance measurement and valuation of these entities. Topics include fundamental accounting techniques and practices, relevant financial reporting standards for the hospitality and real estate industries, subsequent measurement of property, plant, and equipment, and investment property, as well as financing and tax considerations. After taking this course, students are expected to have an in-depth understanding of the financial reporting standards required and be able to apply these standards in the valuation of hospitality and real estate entities.

• Understand the principles of financial reporting required in the hospitality and real estate industries

• Assess the subsequent measurement of property, plant, and equipment, and investment property

• Comprehend the financing and tax considerations

• Apply the principles of financial reporting in conducting the analysis of hospitality and real estate companies
courses.course_syllabus:
• Introduction to accounting techniques and practices

• Hospitality and real estate entity financial reporting

• Acquisition and construction of real estate for hospitality or investment

• F&B revenue and rental income recognition

• Accounting by lessors and lessees

• Finance and tax considerations

• Subsequent value measurement of property

• Disposal of real estate property

• Other reporting issues and disclosures

courses.subject: HTMG

courses.description:
Hospitality and Real Estate (HRE) are dynamic industries that need to constantly change to anticipate the evolving needs of guests, innovation therefore become an inseparable element that must be included in a business process to ensure the quality guest service delivery capability and the competitiveness of the industry. Even though our lives have changed in many ways with the fast development of technology and the evolved Industry 4.0. Innovation is not limited to tangible digital products like smart machine, robotic equipment or greener building, it also includes intangible management process, design or concepts that facilitate efficient use of human capital, marketing effectiveness and financial viability.

The objective of this course is to leverage industry expertise to facilitate discussion of tangible innovative products or intangible innovative process and system which are applicable to the Hospitality and Real Estate sectors, and assist students identify, evaluate and generate new business opportunities for HRE.

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Essentials for Hospitality and Real Estate Projects
courses.description:
The use of (tactical) project management to deliver projects has been increasing in organizations.

A project can be defined as a temporary endeavor undertaken to create a unique product, service, or result.

Projects are particularly important for real estate and hospitality organizations, as a company’s strategic vision is operationalized through project management, with the transfer of projects to operations.

The ability successfully apply knowledge, skills, tools and techniques to project activities has become an important management competency in the real estate and hospitality industries.

The objective of this course is to equip students with basic understanding of:
- project management concepts, including the project life cycle and the series of phases the project passes through from its start to its completion, and interdependencies.
- project management process groups (initiating, planning, executing, closing and monitoring & controlling).
- project management knowledge areas (integration, scope, schedule, cost, quality, resource, communications, risk, procurement, stakeholder).

In addition to discussing phases in a project, the students will be equipped with a beginner skills, and tool and techniques to understand typical hospitality project environments (new-build hotel, transition, renovation, product extension / upgrade, service upgrade, IT hard- and software implementation) and support general management and the project team in basic planning and executing of relevant project activities.

Finally, the course will explore how Enterprise Environmental Factors (EEFs) influence, constrain, or direct the project.
- Internal to the organization: e.g. culture, structure, and governance; geographic distribution of facilities and resources, infrastructure, resource availability, employee capability, etc.
- External to the organization: e.g. marketplace conditions, social and cultural influences and issues; legal restrictions, government or industry standards, physical environmental elements, etc.

Experiential learning activities such as real-world group projects from the real estate/hospitality fields, active case discussions, and business examples will be offered along with lectures for the purpose of enhancing students’ conceptual understanding and practical skills.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completing this course, students should be able to

* understand a project life cycle and phases.
* understand the project management process groups.
* understand the project management knowledge areas.
* identify interdependencies.
* understand basic scope, schedule, cost and quality tools and techniques.
* understand key performance metrics for project success.
* describe how to manage stakeholder interests and relationships in each phase of the project life cycle.
* understand how Enterprise Environmental Factors facilitate (or hinder) successful project management.
* support effective project management teams.

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Hospitality Strategic Management
courses.description: This course focuses on analysis, planning, change, and implementation issues associated with strategic management. It explores business missions, action plans, and evaluations of a hospitality organization. The emphases are on the value of analysing environments and formulating strategies linked to environmental conditions, and defending against organization weaknesses. Students learn how to engage in high-quality planning by examining situations from the perspectives of the customer, the employee, and management. Cases involving hospitality industry organizations are analysed extensively.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Prerequisites: HTMG1010, HTMG3010 and HTMG3030
courses.learning_outcomes:
1) Business Concepts and Functions
First, students are expected to understand three major theoretical perspectives on strategy formulation and implementation: a) the rational model that focuses on SWOT analysis, industry five-force analysis, and generic competitive strategies, b) the resource-based view of competitive advantage, and c) the stakeholder relationship perspective. Students will learn how to apply these theories to describe and prescribe strategies at both business- and corporate-levels. Second, students need to understand strategic management as a process and learn about the various approaches companies use to expand / diversify their businesses. Finally, students are expected to understand the notion of “fit” (external fit and internal fit) and its importance in strategy formulation and implementation (particularly in the area of designing organizational structures and control systems).

2) Analytical Skills
Business cases, video clips, and simulation exercises are used in this course for the purpose of training students’ analytical skills. Students are expected to apply different theoretical concepts and approaches to solve “real-world” problems. These exercises are also designed to help students understand that there are no “right” or “wrong” answers in strategy making. They need to deal with uncertainty, understand boundary conditions, and develop reasonable, realistic solutions.

3) Attitude / Values
The notion of ethical decision making is incorporated in every topic of this course. Students are required to learn not only the various ethical principles but also their implications in specific decision contexts. They will learn how to evaluate strategic proposals / solutions from different stakeholders’ perspectives and how to balance different interests / resolve conflicts. One significant element of this course is to encourage students to think beyond profits and take into account business ethics and social responsibilities in every strategic decision.

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Real Estate Finance
courses.description:
The turmoil in real estate market, which is one of the largest and most important components of the financial system, could bring a negative impact to the world financial markets. The participants in the real estate market should understand how to evaluate the risk and return concerning various ways of financing. This course exposes students to concepts, theories and industry practice in real estate finance. Topics related to real estate finance, including legal foundation of real estate finance, mortgage loans, secondary mortgage market, mortgage-backed securities and financial leverage, will be discussed. Different alternative real estate financing vehicles, such as real estate investment trust (REIT), will also be examined.

The primary goal of this course is to help students develop the conceptual knowledge and analytical skills of real estate finance, including those related to the institutions and instruments used to finance residential and commercial real estate. After taking this course, the students are expected to understand the importance of different financing methods and be able to make informed real estate finance decisions.

• Critically analyze the use of different financing methods in real estate projects

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Real Estate Economics
courses.description: Real estate, governing one-third of the world’s assets, plays an important role in the capital markets. It is thus crucial to understand how the real estate system works and what economic forces drive the real estate value. In the real estate system, there are two markets (space market and asset market) and one industry (development industry). Space market is essential to the overall real estate system as it governs rents. Changes in rent promptly influence the demand for real estate asset market, and development industry at a later date. This course introduces urban and real estate economics. Urban economics is the primary branch of economics for studying the space market. Topics related to urban economics and real estate space-market analysis will be covered, including four-quadrant model, central place theory, residual theory of land value, the concept of rent, bid-rent analysis, neighborhood life cycle, property life cycle, and demand and supply for real estate space market. After taking this course, students are expected to have in-depth understanding of factors that trigger the functioning of real estate space markets.

• Understand the theories and principles of urban economics that are of paramount importance in making well-informed real estate investment decisions
• Comprehend the economic principles and concepts that determine the land value and rent in the city
• Apply the principles of land value and rent determinants in conducting the real estate market analysis
• Analyze Hong Kong’s and Mainland China’s real estate space markets

Students will identify relevant industry issues and provide policy recommendations. These recommendations are grounded in academic literature and supported by empirical evidence. courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Nurture an academic mind to learn from literature independently and make use of concepts to guide evidence based management
2. Master tools to make concepts operational and tools to collect evidence to substantiate evidence based management
3. Our students will demonstrate an understanding of concepts, theories, and practices pertaining to strategic management of hospitality and real estate (HRE) businesses
4. Our students will apply critical thinking and problem solving skills relevant to strategically managing HRE business
5. Our students will demonstrate effective communication, teamwork and leadership skills applicable to a HRE setting
courses.course_syllabus:
The mission of this course is to show you the intimate relationship between theory and practice, and nurture within you a passion in knowledge pursuit in your daily work. The success of SHTM education hinges on our ability to connect theory to practice. This is a course to enable you to develop a framework to connect theory to practice. There are two specific course objectives:

1. The first objective is to show you how an academic mind shapes an executive mind. We all want to become successful executives. A successful executive is someone who has an executive mind. An executive mind makes effective and efficient decisions. In the meantime, we come to study at a leading university because we want to master the best way to learn how to learn. The best learner is someone who has an academic mind. An academic mind has a firm grasp of literature and knows how to learn from current and historic great minds from different disciplines. When we have an academic mind that can guide our executive mind, we can benefit from literature and make well informed effective and efficient decisions. In this course, we introduce to you a way to see theories through daily actions and to see the relationship between theory and practice. Whenever a practice is highly significant, it pays to go to the root of the practice and make sure that there is a sound theory behind. In the meantime, in implementation, a sound practice will ensure that there is a thorough understanding of the sound theory behind.

Theory and practice are two sides of the same coin. When you have an academic mind to distil your work experience and link it to a theoretical framework, you will become much more powerful to make good use of your experience to accelerate learning. Experience without learning is hardship. Experience with learning generates personal satisfaction and growth. Learning hinges on whether one has an academic mind, the way to develop the ability to learn how to learn. The ideal of education at SHTM is to enable us to see the implication of a theory to improving practices and also to see a theory behind proven best practices. It is only possible when we have an academic mind.

2. The second objective is to show you how to nurture an academic mind, the way to develop your ability to learn how to learn. We enable you to appreciate the beauty of the process to develop a body of scientific knowledge in business and social science in the context of the hospitality and real estate industries. This will give you an orientation to appreciate the contribution to literature from various business and social science disciplines, the challenge of problems in the hospitality and real estate industries, the nature of empirical evidence, the importance of journals, books, magazines and libraries, and the meaning of academic work. Graduates with an academic mind who decide to practise in the industries will be able to make executive decisions with a deep meaning.

Topics included:
• Introduction to Theory Construction
• Theory and Hypothesis
• Creativity and the Generation of Ideas
• Concepts
• Paper Discussion
• Causal Models
• Project Discussion
• Research Method & Data Analyses
• SPSS Workshop & Tutorial

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Real Estate Financial Reporting and Analysis
courses.description: Real estate entities that construct, manage and hold real properties encounter various financial reporting issues that could affect their performance measurement. In real estate industry, the financial reporting is in fact unique and complex. While the industry has its own specific standards, it is mandatory that these entities also need to follow the statutory reporting requirements. This course covers the financial reporting practices adopted in the real estate industry and applies the financial statement analysis techniques in the performance measurement and valuation of real estate entities. Topics include fundamental accounting techniques and practices, relevant financial reporting standards for the real estate industry, subsequent measurement of investment property, as well as real estate financing and tax considerations. After taking this course, students are expected to have an in-depth understanding of the financial reporting standards required and be able to apply these standards in the valuation of real estate entities.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MSc Real Estate
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of the course, the students should be able to:
• Understand the principles of financial reporting required in the real estate industry
• Assess the subsequent measurement of investment property
• Comprehend the real estate financing and tax considerations
• Apply the principles of financial reporting in conducting the analysis of real estate companies
• Analyze the financial reporting of the real estate companies in Hong Kong and globally
courses.course_syllabus:
• Introduction to accounting techniques and practices
• Real estate value chain
• Acquisition and construction of real estate
• Rental income recognition
• Accounting by lessors
• Real estate finance and tax considerations
• Subsequent value measurement of real estate property
• Disposal of real estate property
• Other reporting issues and disclosures

2. Focus group/feedback from students

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: PropTech: Emerging Trends and Opportunities
courses.description: The real estate industry, which accounts for a significant portion of global capital assets, used to be known for relying on traditional practices and lacking capacity for continuous innovation. This, however, has changed in recent years with the fast development of property technology (PropTech) applications. PropTech refers to a wide range of technology-enabled solutions used to optimize all stages of the real estate process, from property design, construction, management to property buying, leasing, and selling. This course helps students understand how the Internet, mobile and digital technologies have changed residential and commercial real estate business operations. Students will learn to compare and evaluate PropTech solutions and understand the business opportunities and potential challenges associated with these new tools/platforms. Real-world cases and examples will be used to discuss the impact of PropTech on real estate practices.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MSc Real Estate

* PropTech in commercial property market

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Leadership and Change Management in Experience Economy
courses.description: The rapidly evolving experience economy requires future leaders to develop systemic thinking about what their organizations can offer to customers, employees, and other stakeholders, as well as how their businesses can positively impact society. Leadership in the experience economy entails the ability to address key challenges specific to experience-driven industries, such as motivating, engaging, and empowering employees; effectively leading team collaboration; initiating and managing organizational change; managing crisis; fostering innovation, to name just a few. This course examines the interplay between leadership, creativity, team dynamics, and organizational change within the context of the experience economy. It connects real-world cases and scenarios of leadership challenges in the experience economy with discussions of recent research on leadership and organizational change management.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MSc Leadership for Experience Economy
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Demonstrate a comprehensive understanding of contemporary leadership theories, practices, and challenges within the context of the experience economy.
2. Apply leadership competencies, including motivating and empowering teams, fostering creativity, and managing changes, to address real-world challenges in experience-driven businesses.
3. Analyze and design effective strategies for initiating, implementing, and sustaining organizational change, considering the complexities of the experience economy.
4. Demonstrate critical thinking and problem-solving skills relevant to experience-driven businesses.
5. Develop and demonstrate collaboration skills in teamwork.
6. Cultivate a mindset that prioritizes ethical decision-making, social responsibility, and systemic thinking to enhance the positive impact of leadership practices on customers, employees, and other stakeholders in the experience economy.
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1-2: Define leadership & conventional wisdom on leadership

Week 3: Overview of contemporary leadership theories: How are they relevant to the experience economy?

Week 4-5: What do leaders do? – The leadership challenge in the experience economy

Week 6: Leading creative people

Week 7-8: Multiple perspectives on organizational change

Week 9-11: Process of managing organizational change in the experience economy

Week 12: Leadership and change management in the experience economy: Emerging issues and challenges

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Finance and Investment in Experience Industries
courses.description: Interest in the experience economy has surged in recent years, covering sectors such as entertainment, tourism, hospitality, and events. The core business model of these industries often involves significant investments in real property, facilities, and infrastructure to generate sustainable returns. This course introduces students to the financial and investment principles underlying the experience industries. It equips students with the knowledge and tools to evaluate financial performance, assess investment opportunities, and manage risk and return in this dynamic sector. Students will learn to build cash flow models, perform investment appraisals, and analyze financing decisions, enabling them to advise businesses on strategic investment choices. The course emphasizes practical application of financial analysis and investment strategy, blending academic theory with real-world case studies from hospitality, tourism, and event enterprises.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MSc Leadership for Experience Economy
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Understand and apply financial management principles within the experience industries.
2. Construct and analyze cash flows and financial models for projects in tourism, hospitality, entertainment, vents and other experience industries.
3. Evaluate investment returns, cost of capital, and risks specific to experience industry contexts.
4. Formulate financing strategies and assess funding sources for industry projects.
5. Communicate financial insights effectively in professional contexts.
6. Demonstrate ethical awareness in financial and investment decision-making.

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Hospitality Asset Valuation and Management
courses.description: The hospitality sector encompasses a wide range of industry segments that are closely tied to the daily experience of all members of the society. These industries are typically experience-oriented such as lodging, food and beverages, tourism, sports, event planning and exhibitions, to name just a few. At the same time, engaging in these services requires considerable capital investment in the provision of hardware: hotels, bars and restaurants, exhibition and convention centres, theme parks, golf courses, clubs and gaming facilities etc. They are part and parcel to the success of an experience economy. This course focuses on the valuation and management of hospitality-related asset portfolios. Asset valuation is an integral part of business operations as it facilitates the financing, taxation, statutory reporting and market transaction of assets. It is a good performance yardstick to measure certain aspects of business success and viability. Meanwhile, portfolio asset management is a key skill that can contribute to asset value growth. Superior management and optimal capital structures can greatly enhance portfolio values and returns to investment.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MSc Leadership for Experience Economy
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1 Real estate asset and space markets; segmentation of hospitality assets

Week 2 Real estate valuation: Principles, valuation standards and professional guidelines

Week 3 Valuation methodologies (1): Comparison method, Income method (capitalization)

Week 4 Valuation methodologies (2): Income method (DCF approach)

Week 5 Valuation methodologies (3): Residual approach; Profits method and Replacement cost method

Week 6 Valuation for hospitality assets – adoption of appropriate method, financial statement analysis

Week 7 Hospitality investment: Consideration, structure, styles

Week 8 Investment underwriting – DCF structure and construction

Week 9 Listed investment strategies – top-down and bottom-up approach

Week 10 Risk return analysis, Modern Portfolio Theory (1)

Week 11 Modern Portfolio Theory (2)

Week 12 Asset management skills and technique

Week 13 Final exam

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Digital Transformation in Experience Economy
courses.description: In an increasingly digital and experience-rich world, the ability to transform and adapt to new technologies is crucial for businesses to survive and thrive. This course focuses on the intersection of digital transformation and the experience economy, where companies’ effectiveness in improving customer experiences is at the forefront of value creation. Students will explore the key principles and strategies for successfully navigating digital transformation in the unique landscape of the experience economy. They will learn to identify opportunities for leveraging digital technologies to create memorable and valuable customer experiences. Topics covered include but are not limited to understanding the experience economy and its impact on business operations; the role of digital technologies in creating and enhancing customer experiences; strategies for building organizational capabilities in implementing digital transformation initiatives; emerging technologies and their potential applications in the experience economy, such as AI, IoT, and other immersive technologies; and measuring the success of digital transformation efforts and their impact on customer experience and business performance. By the end of the course, students will be well-prepared to contribute to the digital transformation journeys of businesses and to help shape the future of the experience economy.

CLO 2: *Analyze and build customer experiences to maximize digital transformation efficiency and effectiveness.*

CLO 3: *Analyze and understand external market factors and consumer psychology relating to customer experience and digital transformation trends and preferences.*

CLO 4: *Develop and deliver effective narratives and communications to convey the digital transformation means and ends to customers and CMOs.*

CLO 5: *Develop effective teamwork and leadership skills.*

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: Experience Innovation
courses.description: In an era where exceptional service experiences are paramount, this course explores how organizations can transform their service offerings through innovative practices and technologies. Students will learn to shift from traditional product-centric approaches to dynamic, service-centric models that prioritize customer satisfaction and loyalty. The course integrates insights from diverse fields such as psychology and behavioral economics, providing a rich theoretical foundation for understanding and influencing consumer behavior. Students will delve into cutting-edge innovations, including automation, artificial intelligence (AI), and immersive realities (AR/VR), examining their potential to enhance service delivery and create memorable customer interactions. Through a semester-long project and critical analysis of modern business cases, students will develop strategies to design, prototype, and validate technologically-infused service experiences, ultimately learning how to foster a culture of innovation and position their organizations as leaders in a competitive marketplace.

courses.subject: HTMG
courses.title: HR Analytics Mastery in Hospitality
courses.description:
In today's evolving business landscape, the hospitality industry faces unique challenges such as talent shortages, technological advancements, evolving guest expectations, and supply chain disruptions. These challenges necessitate innovative solutions to maintain competitiveness and ensure operational efficiency. HR analytics is a key solution in this context.
HR analytics is essential in the hospitality industry for several reasons. It optimizes workforce performance by identifying performance trends, training needs, and areas for improvement. This ensures that employees are well-equipped to meet customer expectations, which is crucial in a customer-centric industry like hospitality.
This course covers HR analytics concepts specific to the hospitality industry, including descriptive, predictive, and prescriptive analytics, HR lifecycle metrics, training, rewards, and basic statistical methods. Key topics include HR metrics and KPIs, data collection and management, employee lifecycle analytics, diversity and inclusion, strategic workforce planning, and ethical considerations in HR analytics. Students will engage in hands-on projects and case studies specific to the hospitality industry, applying analytical techniques to real-world HR challenges that hotels, restaurants, and other hospitality businesses.
By the end of this course, students will be equipped to drive impactful HR strategies within their organizations, leveraging data analytics to deliver superior outcomes. This course seeks to enhance students' analytical skills and make data-driven decisions in the hospitality sector.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MSc Leadership for Experience Economy
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Critically analyze complex HR datasets from the hospitality industry to identify trends, correlations, and performance gaps using descriptive analytics techniques.
2. Apply and integrate predictive and prescriptive analytics models to forecast workforce needs and recommend evidence-based HR strategies for hospitality operations.
3. Design and implement data-driven HR dashboards and KPI frameworks that support strategic workforce planning and operational decision-making in hospitality organizations.
4. Evaluate ethical considerations, diversity, and inclusion principles in the application of HR analytics, demonstrating sound judgment in decision-making and policy development.
5. Communicate and justify analytical insights and predictive models to stakeholders through data visualization and storytelling, influencing strategic HR decisions in hospitality businesses.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Foundations of HRM and HR Analytics
2. Strategic HRM & Data Management (HR metrics, KPIs, HRIS, data governance and compliance
3. Job Analysis & Workforce Planning
4. Recruitment Analytics (recruitment funnel, data tracking, assessment tools, predictive validity, recruitment effectiveness analysis)
5. Selection Analytics & Legal Considerations (structured scoring, reliability and validity in selection tools, bias detection in selection algorithms)
6. Training and development analytics (performance data, evaluate training effectiveness and learning analytics)
7. Performance Management & Predictive Analytics (appraisal metrics, performance forecasting, visualizing performance trends)
8. Retention & Turnover Analytics (turnover analysis, retention strategies, attrition risk prediction
9. Rewards & Compensation Analytics (linking rewards with performance data, data-driven incentive system, compensation analytics)
10. Benefits & Employee Satisfaction (Legal requirements, Survey analytics)
11. Employee Relations & Well-being Analytics
12. HR data visualization and storytelling (designing dashboards for HR metrics, report, visualization tools)

Students will analyse ESG challenges and real-world practices, culminating in a group project where they propose actionable ESG solutions for partner organizations. courses.description: To monitor students' progress in thesis and research.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MPhil-PhD Business Administration
courses.learning_outcomes: The goal of this course is to monitor students’ progress in thesis and research. Upon completing this course, students will develop a skill to summarize the essential ingredients of a paper in a concise way, to develop an original research topic, and to empirically execute the idea using what they have learned in other seminar courses.
courses.course_syllabus: To be assigned by course teacher
courses.feedback_evaluation: Class evaluation will be conducted at the end of the course
courses.required_readings:
Depending on the research topic, students are advised to read from the recommended list of journals:
Annuals of Tourism Research
Cornell Hospitality Quarterly
International Journal of Hospitality Management
Journal of Hospitality and Tourism Research
Journal of Consumer Research
Journal of Marketing

Academy of Management Journal
